figures.caption: Fig. 12: Barrett Hand graph construction and qualitative grasping examples.
Fig. 5: Simulated grasps of training hands on 5 diverse objects.
Fig. 11: Representative failure cases on the three unseen hands. (a) LEAP, (b) Inspire, (c) Revo2.
Fig. 10: Training curves in simulation for our MAGCN policy.

Fig. 2: Universal hand representation. (a) Morphology-Aligned State Graph Representation: nodes correspond to anatomical units, edges follow kinematic chains, yielding a hand-agnostic semantic graph structure. (b) Schematic of three motion primitives (Flexion, Abduction, Axial Rotation) on a Schunk hand, showing their physical motion effects at representative joints.
Fig. 8: Joint-to-primitive mapping for all hand embodiments used in this work. Each URDF joint is assigned to one motion primitive.
TABLE IV: Training and architecture hyperparameters.
TABLE III: Real-world zero-shot grasping success rate on unseen dexterous hands.

Fig. 3: Architecture of DexGrasp-Zero. At each time step t: (a) Morphology-Aligned Graph Encoder encodes hand-object state into node features Xh node,t and global feature xh g,t using a hand-specific graph (adjacency Ah); a GCN with per-layer physical priors produces embeddings Eh node,t and Eh g,t. (b) Physical Property Encoder parses hand URDF to build a physical graph Gh physical (joint limits, link lengths, etc.) and an activation mask Mh activation, encoded into Eh p and fused into every GCN layer. (c) Decoder outputs motion primitives αh prim: wrist 6-DoF commands from Eh g,t and wrist features, and joint actions from masked node embeddings; the latter are mapped via hand-specific Mh to executable joint commands αh physical,t.
Fig. 6: Morphology-aligned graph construction results for all hand embodiments used in this work.
